title: Solid Rocket Motors for Missile Defense: Challenges and Opportunities for Expanding the Industrial Base
gist: Abstract/landing summary (full report is a separate download) of a CSIS report by Wes Rumbaugh and Tom Karako on scaling the US solid-rocket-motor (SRM) and missile-interceptor industrial base. The captured text is only the executive summary, so insights are limited to the framing: missiles are now 'weapons of choice', inventories are strained, and several procurement levers could expand capacity. Notably sponsored by Raytheon, Ursa Major, and X-Bow.

insights.claim: Heavy US missile expenditures, even before Operation Epic Fury, have raised concern about the depth, resilience, and production rate of US air-and-missile-defense interceptor inventories, especially for solid rocket motors.
insights.speaker: Wes Rumbaugh and Tom Karako
insights.so_what: Confirms a structural demand-pull for interceptor and SRM production capacity; bullish backdrop for missile-component suppliers.
insights.quote: The increasing use of missiles as “weapons of choice” has created significant urgency behind efforts to strengthen the air and missile defense interceptor industrial base, particularly for key components such as solid rocket motors.

insights.claim: CSIS identifies multiyear procurement agreements, direct-to-supplier investments, alternative acquisition pathways, and leveraging allies/partners as the main tools to overcome cyclical demand, complex supply chains, and misaligned government-industry incentives in SRM production.
insights.speaker: Wes Rumbaugh and Tom Karako
insights.so_what: Watch for multiyear procurement and direct-to-supplier capital as catalysts for SRM suppliers' revenue visibility.

insights.claim: The report was funded by Raytheon Technologies, Ursa Major, and X-Bow, all of which are SRM / propulsion players, indicating these firms are positioning publicly for an industrial-base expansion they would benefit from.
insights.speaker: Wes Rumbaugh and Tom Karako
insights.so_what: Identifies named beneficiaries (incl. private propulsion startups Ursa Major and X-Bow) lobbying the missile-base buildout narrative.
insights.quote: This report was made possible by the generous support of Raytheon Technologies, Ursa Major, and X-Bow
